content.text: Overview
An alert without a runbook is an unfinished alert. The notification wakes an engineer at 3 AM; the runbook tells them what to do before they are fully awake. The link between the two must be
automatic, direct, and pre-filtered
— the engineer clicks one link and sees the right dashboard scoped to the alert's time range, environment, and affected service.

This topic covers the mechanics of embedding runbook links in Prometheus alert rules, the standard four-section runbook structure for GenAI services, pre-filtered Grafana URL patterns, and the CI gate that enforces the linking standard.

attrs.alt: Alert → Alertmanager → notification → runbook → resolution flow with four runbook sections

content.text: The runbook_url Annotation
Prometheus alert rules support an
annotations
block that is forwarded unchanged through Alertmanager to notification receivers. The
runbook_url
annotation is the canonical field for the runbook link:

groups:
  - name: genai_availability
    rules:
      - alert: GenAIHighErrorRateFastBurn
        expr: |
          (
            sum(rate(genai_request_errors_total{env="prod"}[5m]))
            / sum(rate(genai_requests_total{env="prod"}[5m]))
          ) > 0.025
          and
          sum(rate(genai_requests_total{env="prod"}[5m])) > 1
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: critical
          team: genai-oncall
        annotations:
          summary: 'GenAI error rate {{ $value | humanizePercentage }} — 5× SLO burn'
          description: |
            Error rate has been above 2.5% for 5 minutes.
            Env: {{ $labels.env }}  Model: {{ $labels.model }}
          runbook_url: 'https://wiki.example.com/runbooks/genai-high-error-rate'
          dashboard_url: >-
            https://grafana.example.com/d/genai-triage/incident-triage
            ?var-env={{ $labels.env }}&var-model={{ $labels.model }}
            &from=now-1h&to=now
Two annotations serve different purposes.
runbook_url
is the procedure document — what to do.
dashboard_url
is the live view — what to look at. Both should be pre-filtered to avoid the on-call spending the first five minutes locating the right dashboard.
Pre-Filtered Grafana URL Pattern
Grafana's URL format supports template variable injection via
var-<name>=<value>
query parameters. Because Prometheus label values are available in annotations via
{{ $labels.<name> }}
, the dashboard link can be dynamically scoped to the exact environment, model, and team that triggered the alert.
The
from
parameter should be set to a window that includes the pre-incident baseline. For an alert with
for: 5m
, the incident may have started up to 10 minutes before the alert fired. Using
from=now-1h
provides sufficient context without drowning in unrelated history.
For PagerDuty, the custom details field maps directly to Alertmanager annotation values:

# alertmanager/receivers.yaml
receivers:
  - name: genai-pagerduty
    pagerduty_configs:
      - routing_key: '<PD_ROUTING_KEY>'
        severity: '{{ if eq .CommonLabels.severity "critical" }}critical{{ else }}warning{{ end }}'
        summary: '{{ .CommonAnnotations.summary }}'
        description: '{{ .CommonAnnotations.description }}'
        details:
          runbook: '{{ .CommonAnnotations.runbook_url }}'
          dashboard: '{{ .CommonAnnotations.dashboard_url }}'
          env: '{{ .CommonLabels.env }}'
          model: '{{ .CommonLabels.model }}'
          team: '{{ .CommonLabels.team }}'
Standard Runbook Structure

Every GenAI runbook follows a four-section template. The sections are ordered by time urgency: the on-call reads section 1 to understand scope, section 2 to diagnose, section 3 to mitigate, and section 4 if mitigation fails.
Section 1: Impact Statement
The impact statement answers:
what is the user experiencing right now, and how many users are affected?
It must be answerable in under 2 minutes.

## GenAI High Error Rate — Runbook

### 1. Impact Statement

**What users see:** Requests to the GenAI completion API are returning 5xx errors.
Features affected: chat, summarization, search-answer. RAG pipeline may fall back to
keyword-only results.

**Scope check (open these now):**
- [Incident Triage Dashboard](https://grafana.example.com/d/genai-triage?var-env=prod&from=now-1h)
- [Error Rate by Feature](https://grafana.example.com/d/genai-golden?var-env=prod&from=now-1h)
- [Current Error Budget](https://grafana.example.com/d/genai-slo?var-env=prod)

**Severity:** If error rate > 5% → this is a P1. Page the platform team.
If error rate 1–5% → P2, investigate before escalating.
Section 2: Common Causes
The common causes section is a
decision tree
, not a flat list. Each cause has a distinguishing signal the on-call can check in the linked dashboards:

### 2. Common Causes

Check in this order:

**A. Provider outage or degradation (most common)**
- Signal: `genai_request_errors_total{error_type="provider_error"}` is elevated
- Check: [OpenAI Status](https://status.openai.com) / [Anthropic Status](https://status.anthropic.com)
- Action: → Section 3A (fallback model)

**B. Rate limit exhaustion**
- Signal: `error_type="rate_limit_exceeded"` is non-zero
- Check: Token rate vs quota panel in triage dashboard
- Action: → Section 3B (reduce traffic)

**C. Prompt regression after deploy**
- Signal: Error started near a recent deployment (check deploy timeline annotation)
- Check: Which feature deployed in the last 2 hours? `error_type="content_filter"` elevated?
- Action: → Section 3C (rollback feature flag)

**D. Application bug**
- Signal: `error_type="internal_error"` elevated; other error types normal
- Check: Application error logs in Loki
- Action: → Section 3D (rollback service)
Section 3: Immediate Mitigation
Mitigation is
not
the permanent fix. It is the fastest action that stops user harm. Each mitigation branch should be a copy-paste command or a two-click UI action:

### 3. Immediate Mitigation

**3A — Fallback to backup model (provider outage)**
```bash
# Switch to backup provider via feature flag
feature-flags set genai.completion.provider=anthropic --env prod
# Verify traffic shifts within 60 seconds:
# watch the 'Provider' breakdown in the triage dashboard
```
Revert: `feature-flags set genai.completion.provider=openai --env prod`

**3B — Reduce token budget (rate limit)**
```bash
# Halve the per-request token budget to reduce TPM consumption
feature-flags set genai.completion.max_tokens=512 --env prod
# This degrades response length but prevents hard failures
```
Revert after quota resets (check provider dashboard for reset time).

**3C — Rollback feature flag (prompt regression)**
```bash
# Identify the feature that deployed:
git log --oneline -5 origin/main
# Disable the feature flag for that feature:
feature-flags set <feature>.enabled=false --env prod
```

**3D — Rollback service (application bug)**
```bash
# Get the previous stable image tag:
kubectl rollout history deployment/genai-completion -n production
# Roll back:
kubectl rollout undo deployment/genai-completion -n production
# Wait for rollout:
kubectl rollout status deployment/genai-completion -n production
```
Section 4: Escalation and Resolution

### 4. Escalation

If mitigation has not resolved the alert within **15 minutes**:

| Condition | Escalate to | Contact |
|---|---|---|
| Provider outage confirmed | Platform team | `@platform-oncall` on Slack |
| Quota exhausted, cannot reduce traffic | Engineering manager | `@eng-manager` |
| Application rollback failed | Service owner | See OWNERS file in repo |

**Open an incident channel:**
```
/incident create title="GenAI High Error Rate" severity=P1
```
This creates `#inc-genai-YYYYMMDD` and pages the incident commander.

### Resolution Verification

Before closing the alert:
1. Error rate has been < 0.5% for at least 10 minutes (check triage dashboard)
2. Alert resolved in Alertmanager (not just acknowledged)
3. Post-incident ticket opened: link root cause and mitigation taken

**Post-incident ticket template:**
- Root cause: [one sentence]
- Time to detect: [alert firing time - first user impact time]
- Time to mitigate: [mitigation action time - alert firing time]
- Action items: [≥1 concrete follow-up to prevent recurrence]
CI Enforcement of runbook_url
A runbook link that exists in the rule file but points to a 404 is as bad as no link. The CI pipeline enforces two things: the annotation exists, and the URL returns 200.

# .github/workflows/alert-lint.yml
name: Alert Rule Lint
on: [pull_request]
jobs:
  lint:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Check runbook_url annotations
        run: python3 scripts/check_runbook_links.py
      - name: Validate alert YAML syntax
        run: |
          for f in alerts/**/*.yaml; do
            promtool check rules "$f" || exit 1
          done
Runbook Discovery and Index
As the alert library grows, an on-call engineer needs to find runbooks quickly — not just via alert links but also when preemptively investigating a trend. Maintain a
runbook index
as a single YAML file that maps alert names to metadata:

# runbooks/index.yaml
runbooks:
  - alert: GenAIHighErrorRateFastBurn
    url: https://wiki.example.com/runbooks/genai-high-error-rate
    owner: genai-oncall
    last_tested: "2025-10-01"   # date of last game-day exercise
    related_alerts:
      - GenAIHighErrorRateSlowBurn
      - GenAIProviderErrorSurge
    tags: [availability, provider]

  - alert: GenAIHighLatencyFastBurn
    url: https://wiki.example.com/runbooks/genai-high-latency
    owner: genai-oncall
    last_tested: "2025-09-15"
    related_alerts:
      - GenAIStreamingTTFTHigh
    tags: [latency, streaming]

  - alert: GenAIRateLimitSaturation
    url: https://wiki.example.com/runbooks/genai-rate-limit
    owner: genai-oncall
    last_tested: "2025-10-10"
    related_alerts:
      - GenAIQuotaPreWarning
    tags: [saturation, quota]
The
last_tested

date is updated each time the runbook is exercised in a game day or real incident. A CI check can flag runbooks that have not been tested in more than 90 days — stale runbooks are nearly as dangerous as missing ones.
Game Day Exercises
A runbook is only as good as the last time someone followed it under pressure.
Game days
are scheduled exercises where the team intentionally triggers an alert in staging and follows the runbook from start to finish, measuring time to diagnose and time to mitigate.
Exercise
Alert triggered
Inject method
Success criteria
Provider outage sim
GenAIHighErrorRateFastBurn
Set provider URL to a dead endpoint via feature flag
Fallback model enabled in < 10 min
Rate limit sim
GenAIRateLimitSaturation
Reduce quota metric to near-zero in staging
Traffic reduced via token budget in < 5 min
Prompt regression sim
GenAIContentFilterSurge
Deploy a prompt that triggers content filter 30% of the time
Feature flag disabled, error rate drops in < 10 min
Quality drift sim
GenAIEvalScoreDrift
Switch to a known-low-quality model in staging
Drift identified and escalated to ML team in < 30 min
After each game day, update the runbook with any steps that were missing or unclear, and update the
last_tested
date in the runbook index. Track
time to mitigation
across exercises to see whether on-call readiness is improving.
The primary purpose of a game day is not to test the
system
but to test the
on-call process
. A game day that finds a gap in the runbook is a successful game day.
Runbook Maintenance Standards
Runbooks must be kept current. A set of standards enforces freshness:
Every alert rule merged to
main
must have a
runbook_url
annotation — CI rejects rules without one
Every runbook URL must return HTTP 200 — CI rejects broken links
Runbooks must be exercised in a game day within 90 days of creation — the index
last_tested
field is the record
After every production incident, the runbook is reviewed and updated — the post-mortem template includes a
runbook update
section
Runbooks are owned by the same team as the alert — the OWNERS file applies to both
Summary
Alert-to-runbook linking is the last step that converts a metric threshold into an operational procedure. The standards are:
Every alert rule includes runbook_url and dashboard_url annotations; CI enforces presence and reachability
Dashboard URLs are pre-filtered with Grafana template variables from Prometheus label values
Runbooks follow a four-section structure: impact statement, common causes (decision tree), mitigation branches (copy-paste commands), and escalation path
A runbook index YAML tracks owner, last-tested date, and related alerts for discovery
Game days exercise runbooks quarterly; the last_tested date is the record of readiness
Post-incident reviews update runbooks before the post-mortem is closed
